section.title: Vows and Tithes
section.sectionRationale: This closing appendix to Leviticus regulates the valuation and redemption of vows, persons, animals, houses, and land dedicated to the LORD, along with the tithe, ending the book with the reminder that what is vowed or dedicated to God is a serious, binding commitment.
section.thesis: God regulates the valuation and redemption of vows, whether of persons, animals, houses, or land, and affirms that the tithe already belongs to Him, teaching Israel that what is dedicated or vowed to the LORD is a serious, binding commitment, not a casual gesture.
section.frameNote: Four movements: valuation of persons dedicated by vow, set by age and sex, with provision for the poor; regulations for vowed or consecrated animals, clean and unclean; the valuation and redemption of consecrated houses and land; and the closing statement that the tithe belongs to the LORD and cannot be casually substituted.

units.label: The valuation of persons
units.note: by age and sex, with mercy for the poor

verses.text: Then the LORD said to Moses,
“Speak to the Israelites and say to them, ‘When someone makes a special {{nadar|vow}} to the LORD involving the value of persons,
if the valuation concerns a male from twenty to sixty years of age, then your {{erekh|valuation}} shall be fifty shekels of silver, according to the sanctuary shekel.
Or if it is a female, then your valuation shall be thirty shekels.
And if the person is from five to twenty years of age, then your valuation for the male shall be twenty shekels, and for the female ten shekels.
Now if the person is from one month to five years of age, then your valuation for the male shall be five shekels of silver, and for the female three shekels of silver.
And if the person is sixty years of age or older, then your valuation shall be fifteen shekels for the male and ten shekels for the female.
But if the one making the vow is too poor to pay the valuation, he is to present the person before the priest, who shall set the value according to what the one making the vow can afford.
If he vows an animal that may be brought as an offering to the LORD, any such animal given to the LORD shall be holy.
He must not replace it or exchange it, either good for bad or bad for good. But if he does substitute one animal for another, both that animal and its substitute will be holy.
But if the vow involves any of the unclean animals that may not be brought as an offering to the LORD, the animal must be presented before the priest.
The priest shall set its value, whether high or low; as the priest values it, the price will be set.
If, however, the owner decides to redeem the animal, he must add a fifth to its value.

media.alt: A scale weighing silver shekels against land and house
media.caption: Every vowed house or field is valued fairly by the priest, tied to the years remaining until Jubilee.

verses.text: Now if a man consecrates his house as holy to the LORD, then the priest shall value it either as good or bad. The price will stand just as the priest values it.
But if he who consecrated his house redeems it, he must add a fifth to the assessed value, and it will belong to him.
If a man consecrates to the LORD a parcel of his land, then your valuation shall be proportional to the seed required for it, fifty shekels of silver for every homer of barley seed.
If he consecrates his field during the Year of Jubilee, the price will stand according to your valuation.
But if he consecrates his field after the Jubilee, the priest is to calculate the price in proportion to the years left until the next Year of Jubilee, so that your valuation will be reduced.
And if the one who consecrated the field decides to redeem it, he must add a fifth to the assessed value, and it shall belong to him.
If, however, he does not redeem the field, or if he has sold it to another man, it may no longer be redeemed.
When the field is released in the Jubilee, it will become holy, like a field devoted to the LORD; it becomes the property of the priests.
Now if a man consecrates to the LORD a field he has purchased, which is not a part of his own property,
then the priest shall calculate for him the value up to the Year of Jubilee, and the man shall pay the assessed value on that day as a sacred offering to the LORD.
In the Year of Jubilee the field shall return to the one from whom it was bought, the original owner of the land.
Every valuation will be according to the sanctuary shekel, twenty gerahs to the shekel.

units.label: Firstborn, devoted things, and the tithe

verses.text: But no one may consecrate a firstborn of the livestock, because a firstborn belongs to the LORD. Whether it is an ox or a sheep, it is the LORD’s.
But if it is among the unclean animals, then he may redeem it according to your valuation and add a fifth of its value. If it is not redeemed, then it shall be sold according to your valuation.
Nothing that a man sets apart to the LORD from all he owns, whether a man, an animal, or his inherited land, can be sold or redeemed; everything so {{cherem|devoted}} is most holy to the LORD.
No person set apart for destruction may be ransomed; he must surely be put to death.
Thus any {{maaser|tithe}} from the land, whether from the seed of the land or the fruit of the trees, belongs to the LORD; it is holy to the LORD.
If a man wishes to redeem part of his tithe, he must add a fifth to its value.
Every tenth animal from the herd or flock that passes under the shepherd’s rod will be holy to the LORD.

He must not inspect whether it is good or bad, and he shall not make any substitution. But if he does make a substitution, both the animal and its substitute shall become holy; they cannot be redeemed.’”
These are the commandments that the LORD gave to Moses for the Israelites on Mount Sinai.

nadar.gloss: A vow, a voluntary, binding promise made to God. Because a vow was entirely voluntary to make but not voluntary to break once made, Ecclesiastes 5:4-5 warns that it is better not to vow at all than to vow and fail to pay.

erekh.gloss: Valuation, an assessed monetary worth. Used throughout this chapter for the fixed redemption price of a person, animal, house, or field dedicated to God, giving the whole chapter its traditional Hebrew name, Arakhin.

cherem.gloss: Something devoted or irrevocably set apart, often to destruction in contexts of holy war, but here of anything so completely dedicated to the LORD that it can never be sold, redeemed, or reclaimed by its former owner.

maaser.gloss: Tithe, a tenth. Unlike a vow, which was voluntary before it was made, the tithe already and inherently belongs to the LORD by definition, which is why it cannot simply be replaced with an inferior substitute.

basic.context: This closing chapter regulates the redemption price for anything vowed or dedicated to God, people, animals, houses, and land, and reaffirms that the tithe already belongs to the LORD by right, closing Leviticus with a final statement on the seriousness of vows.
basic.hermeneutics: This is old covenant civil and cultic law regulating Israel's specific vow and tithe system, tied to the sanctuary economy and the Jubilee calendar. It does not directly govern Christian giving practices today, though its underlying seriousness about promises made to God, and its provision for the poor, continue to instruct.
basic.originalLanguages: A nadar, vow, was voluntary to make but binding once spoken, requiring a fixed erekh, valuation, to redeem. Anything cherem, devoted irrevocably, could never be redeemed at all, and the ma'aser, tithe, was different still, since it already belonged to God by definition rather than by a person's own choice.
basic.typology: The provision for the poor, whose valuation the priest sets according to what they can afford, anticipates the gospel, where Christ Himself pays the redemption price for those who could never afford it. The thirty shekel valuation for a woman is echoed in Zechariah's ironic thirty pieces of silver, the price at which the true Shepherd is later betrayed.
basic.christ: Where this chapter required a person to pay their own valuation, or have it reduced according to their poverty, the gospel goes further still: Christ Himself pays the entire redemption price for people who could never afford it, not with silver but with His own precious blood, 1 Peter 1:18-19.
basic.god: God takes vows and dedicated things with total seriousness, refusing careless substitution or empty promises made to Him, yet He also builds in mercy: the poor are valued according to what they can actually pay, showing that His holiness does not exclude compassion for the genuinely unable.
basic.crossReferences: Ecclesiastes 5:4-5 warns it is better not to vow than to vow and fail to pay. Zechariah 11:12-13 records the contemptuous thirty pieces of silver paid for the true Shepherd. Matthew 27:9-10 shows this fulfilled in the price paid for Jesus' betrayal. Ephesians 2:8-9 describes salvation as an unearned gift, echoing the mercy shown to the poor in this chapter.
basic.oneStory: Leviticus closes by insisting that what is promised or dedicated to God matters and must be honored, while still making room for those who cannot pay their own way. That same tension, the seriousness of what is owed to God and mercy for those unable to pay it, is resolved completely in Christ, who pays in full what no sinner could ever afford.

context.literary: This chapter functions as an appendix to Leviticus, addressing the practical question of what happens when vows involving persons, animals, or property need to be valued and, if desired, redeemed, closing the book on a note of concrete, everyday application.
context.historical: Vows dedicating a person's service, an animal, a house, or a field to God were a normal part of ancient Israelite religious life; this chapter provided the fixed, predictable pricing needed to prevent both careless vow-making and exploitative valuation by priests.
context.canonical: This chapter's valuation system, especially the thirty shekel price for a woman, echoes forward into the prophetic and Gospel narrative of the price paid to betray Jesus, tying Leviticus's closing chapter unexpectedly to the passion of Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To establish fixed, fair valuations for redeeming vowed persons, animals, houses, and fields, preventing both careless vow-making and exploitative redemption pricing, while affirming that the tithe already and always belongs to the LORD.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This is old covenant civil and cultic law regulating Israel's specific vow and tithe system, tied to the sanctuary economy and the Jubilee calendar; it does not directly prescribe Christian giving practices today, though its underlying seriousness about promises made to God, and its example of mercy toward those unable to pay, continue to instruct the church.

debates.question: Why does this valuation system assign different amounts to men and women, and to different ages?

views.label: Reflecting average economic labor value, not spiritual worth
views.case: Most interpreters read these figures as pragmatic valuations of expected labor and economic contribution in an ancient agrarian economy, not a statement about the intrinsic spiritual worth of a woman, child, or elderly person before God, since Genesis 1:27 affirms all humanity, male and female, made equally in God's image.

views.label: A hard text that should be held honestly alongside Scripture's affirmation of equal worth
views.case: Some caution against too quickly resolving the discomfort this text raises, since it can be misused to devalue women or the vulnerable if read carelessly, and insist Christians should let the New Testament's clear teaching on equal worth and standing in Christ, Galatians 3:28, govern how this text is read and applied today.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree this valuation system concerned redemption price for a vow, an economic and legal matter, not a divine ranking of human worth, and that Scripture elsewhere is unambiguous that men and women are equally made in God's image and equally precious to Him.

study.originalLanguages: A nadar, vow, was entirely voluntary to make, yet once spoken required a fixed erekh, valuation, to redeem, a distinction Ecclesiastes 5:4-5 later presses hard. Something cherem, devoted irrevocably, could never be redeemed at all under any circumstance, while the ma'aser, tithe, stands apart from both categories, since it already belonged to God by definition rather than by any person's voluntary choice.

typology.type: Provision for the poor who cannot afford the priest's valuation, verse 8
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the gospel, where Christ Himself pays the redemption price for those who could never afford it themselves, Ephesians 2:8-9, 1 Peter 1:18-19.

typology.type: Thirty shekels, the valuation set for a woman aged twenty to sixty
typology.fulfillment: Echoed in Zechariah's ironic thirty pieces of silver, the price at which the true Shepherd of Israel is valued and betrayed, fulfilled when Judas is paid exactly this sum for Jesus, Zechariah 11:12-13, Matthew 27:9-10.

study.christ: This chapter lets the poor pay a reduced valuation set according to what they could actually afford, verse 8, yet the gospel exceeds even this mercy: Christ Himself pays the entire redemption price for people who could never afford any part of their own ransom, not with silver or gold but with His own precious blood, a lamb without blemish or defect, 1 Peter 1:18-19. Remarkably, the thirty shekel valuation this chapter sets for a woman is the same contemptuous sum Zechariah later describes as the wage paid for the true Shepherd of Israel, fulfilled exactly when Judas receives thirty pieces of silver to betray Jesus, Matthew 27:9-10.
study.god: God takes with total seriousness what is vowed or dedicated to Him, refusing careless substitution of a worse animal for a better one, or empty promises never fulfilled, since Ecclesiastes later warns it is better never to vow than to vow and fail to pay. Yet even within this exacting system, God builds in real mercy: the poor are valued according to what they can actually afford, not a rigid standard that would crush them, showing His holiness never excludes compassion for the genuinely unable.

crossReferences.note: It is better not to vow at all than to make a vow and not fulfill it.
Records the contemptuous thirty pieces of silver paid as the shepherd's wage, later fulfilled in the price of Jesus' betrayal.
Records Judas paid thirty pieces of silver to betray Jesus, fulfilling Zechariah's prophecy.
Believers are redeemed not with silver or gold but with the precious blood of Christ.

study.oneStory: Leviticus closes by insisting that what is vowed or dedicated to God is serious and must be honored, while still making merciful room for those who cannot pay their own way. That same tension, the real weight of what is owed to a holy God and mercy for those unable to pay it, is resolved completely in Christ, who pays in full, at infinite cost to Himself, what no sinner could ever afford on their own.

quiz.q: What provision did the law make for someone too poor to pay the standard valuation for a vowed person, according to verse 8?

options.text: The priest would set a value according to what the person making the vow could actually afford.
options.note: Verse 8 states this provision directly.

options.text: The vow was simply canceled with no further obligation.
options.note: Verse 8 describes an adjusted valuation, not a canceled obligation.

options.text: The poor were exempt from ever making vows.
options.note: The text addresses what happens after a vow is made, not a prohibition on the poor making vows.

options.text: No provision is made for the poor in this chapter.
options.note: Verse 8 explicitly provides for this situation.

quiz.q: What was required if someone substituted one vowed animal for another, according to verse 10?

options.text: Both the original animal and its substitute became holy to the LORD.
options.note: Verse 10 states this outcome directly.

options.text: The substitution was simply permitted with no consequence.
options.note: Verse 10 explicitly states a consequence: both animals become holy.

options.text: The person making the substitution was put to death.
options.note: The text specifies both animals become holy, not a capital penalty for this act.

options.text: No rule addresses animal substitution in this chapter.
options.note: Verse 10 explicitly addresses this situation.

quiz.q: What additional amount had to be paid if someone wished to redeem a consecrated house or field, according to verses 15 and 19?

options.text: A fifth added to the assessed value.
options.note: Both verses specify this exact additional amount.

options.text: Double the assessed value.
options.note: The text specifies adding a fifth, not doubling the price.

options.text: No additional amount was required.
options.note: Verses 15 and 19 explicitly require an additional fifth.

options.text: The entire value had to be paid again from scratch.
options.note: The text specifies only an added fifth, not a full repayment.

quiz.q: Why could a firstborn animal not be consecrated by vow, according to verse 26?

options.text: Because a firstborn already belonged to the LORD by right.
options.note: Verse 26 states this reasoning directly.

options.text: Because firstborn animals were considered unclean.
options.note: The reasoning given concerns prior ownership, not ceremonial cleanness.

options.text: No reason is given for this restriction.
options.note: Verse 26 explicitly gives the reason.

options.text: Because firstborn animals were too valuable to give away.
options.note: The stated reason is that the firstborn already belongs to the LORD, not its market value.

quiz.q: How does the thirty shekel valuation for a woman in this chapter connect to the New Testament?

options.text: It echoes the same contemptuous sum, thirty pieces of silver, later paid to Judas to betray Jesus, fulfilling Zechariah's prophecy.
options.note: Zechariah 11:12-13 and Matthew 27:9-10 connect this figure directly.

options.text: It has no connection whatsoever to any New Testament event.
options.note: Zechariah and Matthew draw directly on this same sum and its symbolic weight.

options.text: It describes the price the church later charged for baptism.
options.note: This figure has no connection to any Christian sacramental practice.

options.text: It predicts the exact price of a specific New Testament land purchase.
options.note: The connection concerns Judas' betrayal price, not a land transaction.

questions: This chapter insists that vows made to God are serious and binding, not casual words. Are there promises you have made to God, in prayer, at a significant moment, that you have quietly let slide?

The law made merciful room for those too poor to pay the standard valuation. Where do you need to extend that same kind of realistic, gracious accommodation to someone who is genuinely struggling, rather than holding them to a standard they cannot meet?

The tithe belonged to God by definition, not merely by voluntary choice like a vow. How does recognizing that what you have already belongs to God change the way you think about giving, time, money, or talent?

The thirty shekel valuation for a woman becomes, centuries later, the very price at which the true Shepherd is betrayed. How does seeing Christ willingly enter into that kind of contempt and low valuation deepen your sense of what He was willing to bear for you?

Leviticus closes with careful attention to the small print of vows and valuations. What does this teach you about how seriously God takes the details of your own commitments and follow-through, not just your big, sweeping intentions?

translationNotes.note: The valuations given here concern the redemption price for a vow involving a person's dedicated service, not a statement of a person's spiritual worth; they likely reflect expected economic labor value in an agrarian society.

Devoted translates cherem, a term also used elsewhere in the Old Testament for objects or people set apart for destruction in the context of holy war; here it describes something so completely dedicated to God that, unlike an ordinary vow, it can never be redeemed at any price.
The tithe is described as already belonging to the LORD, in contrast to the voluntary vows discussed earlier in the chapter, which were optional to make though binding once spoken.
